coredata.dc:title: Dual-colored carbon dots-based ratiometric fluorescent sensor for high-precision detection of alkaline phosphatase activity
coredata.dc:creator: Shao, Kang
Yang, Ye
Ye, Shiyi
Gu, Danyu
Wang, Tao
Teng, Yuanjie
Shen, Zhenlu
Pan, Zaifa
coredata.dc:description: Probing the level and activity of alkaline phosphatases (ALP) is of great significance for biomedical research on cellular functions and clinical diagnosis of cancers. Herein, a novel dual-colored carbon dots (CDs)-based ratiometric fluorescent sensor was constructed to accomplish high sensitive and accurate determination of ALP relyed on the difference of fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) between blue light emitted CDs (B-CDs)-MnO2 nanohybrid and yellow light emitted CDs (Y-CDs)-MnO2 nanohybrid. The ratiometric fluorescent sensor enabled sensitive discrimination of ALP against other enzymes in a linear range of 0.1–500 U/L with a limit of detection of 0.02 U/L. The lower error and signal fluctuation, more satisfactory LODs and higher R value (R2 = 0.99552) of the ratiometric sensing platform than single signal detection mode (R2 = 0.85231; R2 = 0.64260) indicated the superiority of the ratiometric fluorescence detection mode.Besides, the excellent analytical performance towards ALP in biological system demonstrated the potential application in clinical diagnosis.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Dual-colored carbon dots
Alkaline phosphatases
Ratiometric
Fluorescent sensor
Fluorescence resonance energy transfer

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Detection of ALP activity in aqueous solution
Characterization of CDs and MnO2 nanosheets
Investigation of the optical properties of B-CDs, Y-CDs, B/Y-CDs mixture and B/Y-CDs-MnO2
Analytical performance of dual-colored based ratiometric fluorescent sensing toward AA
Analytical performance of dual-colored based ratiometric fluorescent sensing toward ALP
Selectivity and stability of dual-colored based ratiometric fluorescent sensors
Standard addition recovery experiments of the ALP in three negative human serums

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: UV–Vis absorption (Abs), fluorescence excitation (Ex) and emission (Em) spectra of (A) B-CDs and (B) Y-CDs (Insets: photographs under daylight (a) and 365 nm UV light (b)); (C) UV–Vis absorption spectra of MnO2 nanosheets (Insets: photograph under daylight); TEM images of (D) B-CDs, (E) Y-CDs and (F) MnO2 nanosheets (Insets: TEM images with higher magnification).
XPS full scan survey, C1s, N1s and O1s spectra of (A) B-CDs and (B) Y-CDs.

FL spectra of (A) B-CDs, (B) Y-CDs, (C) B/Y-CDs, (F) B/Y-CDs-MnO2, (I) B/Y-CDs-MnO2-AA at different excitation wavelengths; (D) FL spectra of B/Y-CDs with different dosages of B-CDs; (E) FL spectra of B/Y-CDs and UV–Vis absorption spectra of MnO2 nanosheets; (G) FL spectra of B/Y-CDs-MnO2 with different dosages of MnO2 nanosheets (Insets: corresponding variation curve); (H) FL spectra of B/Y-CDs, B/Y-CDs-MnO2 and B/Y-CDs-MnO2-AA (Insets: corresponding histogram).

(A) FL spectra and (D) Normalized FL spectra of B/Y-CDs-MnO2-AA in the presence of different CAA; The relationship (B) between FB at 443 nm and CAA, (C) between FY at 539 nm and CAA, r (E) between FB/FY and CAA; (F) between FB/FY and log CAA. (FB, FY, FB/FY and CAA represent the fluorescence intensity of B-CDs, fluorescence intensity of Y-CDs, fluorescence intensity ratios of B-CDs and Y-CDs and concentration of AA, respectively).

(A) Fluorescence spectra of B/Y-CDs-MnO2 sensing system in the presence of different concentrations of ALP (The inset is the drawing of partial enlargement). (B) The relationship between FB/FY and the concentrations of ALP (0.1–500 U/L) (The inset is their corresponding linear relationship). (C) Specificity of the sensing system against ALP, α-amylase, bovine serum albumin, pepsin and parenzyme.
Schematic illustration of dual-colored carbon dots-based ratiometric fluorescent sensing of ALP.
Standard addition recovery experiments of the ALP.

head.ce:title: Dual-colored carbon dots-based ratiometric fluorescent sensor for high-precision detection of alkaline phosphatase activity

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemical Engineering, Zhejiang University of Technology, Hangzhou, 310014, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: . College of Chemical Engineering, Zhejiang University of Technology, Hangzhou 310014, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Life Science, Yangtze University, Jingzhou, Hubei, 434023, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: . College of Life Science, Yangtze University, Jingzhou, Hubei 434023, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Probing the level and activity of alkaline phosphatases (ALP) is of great significance for biomedical research on cellular functions and clinical diagnosis of cancers. Herein, a novel dual-colored carbon dots (CDs)-based ratiometric fluorescent sensor was constructed to accomplish high sensitive and accurate determination of ALP relyed on the difference of fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) between blue light emitted CDs (B-CDs)-MnO2 nanohybrid and yellow light emitted CDs (Y-CDs)-MnO2 nanohybrid. The ratiometric fluorescent sensor enabled sensitive discrimination of ALP against other enzymes in a linear range of 0.1–500 U/L with a limit of detection of 0.02 U/L. The lower error and signal fluctuation, more satisfactory LODs and higher R value (R2 = 0.99552) of the ratiometric sensing platform than single signal detection mode (R2 = 0.85231; R2 = 0.64260) indicated the superiority of the ratiometric fluorescence detection mode.Besides, the excellent analytical performance towards ALP in biological system demonstrated the potential application in clinical diagnosis.

ce:section.ce:para: Alkaline phosphatases (ALP), a category of zinc glycoproteins contains of 449 amino acids with the molecular weight of ∼56 KDa, are the essential enzymes widely existed in organisms from bacteria to eukaryotes. They can catalyze the hydrolysis of many phosphate molecules such as nucleic acids, proteins, peptides etc. [1,2]. Accumulated clinical diagnosis data demonstrated that ALP played essential roles in regulating many cellular functions [3]. The normal level of ALP in human serum is in a range of 46–190 U L−1 [4]. The abnormal level and activity of ALP are often associated with bone disease, intestinal diseases, heart failure, liver dysfunction, breast and ovarian cancers [5]. Over the past few decades, considerable research efforts have been devoted to develop convenient and reliable analytical techniques of ALP, including enzyme linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA), fluorescence, electrochemistry and surface enhanced raman spectroscopy (SERS) methods [6–10]. Among them, fluorescence analysis holds a dominant position in the real-time and on-site detection relying on the advantages of simplicity, high sensitivity and good flexibility [11,12]. Up to date, available fluorophores range from organic dyes and metal chelates to luminescent nanocrystals have been successfully applied to construct probes or sensors of ALP and achieved high responses to targets [13]. Nonetheless, most of traditional organic dyes, heavy metal chelates and quantum dots (QDs) always suffer from the drawbacks of either poor photostability or high toxicity, which have intrinsically hampered their further biological and medical applications.

Since carbon dots (CDs) were first discovered by Scrivens's group in 2004 year, they have been attracting particularly attentions due to their outstanding merits of high quantum yield (QY), good biocompatibility and photostability as well as excellent water solubility [14,15]. Compared with other fluorescent materials, they have more abundant raw sources ranging from laboratory-produced chemicals to natural products and synthetic approaches including arc discharge, laser ablation, pyrolysis, electrochemical oxidation, solvothermal reactions, microwave treatments and ultrasonic method [16–18]. Thus, widely application of CDs in the field of bioimaging, therapeutics, biosensors and opto-electronic devices have been carried out [19–22]. For fluorescence sensing, based on the fluorescence changes (intensity, wavelength, lifetime, anisotropy etc.) related to the concentration of different analytes, CDs-based probes or sensors have achieved excellent analytical performance towards various analytes involving ions, small molecules, DNA, protein, cells and bacteria [23–27]. Even so, two major obstacles exerted negative influence on their sensitivity and accuracy: (i) the blue-to green-light emitted CDs would be interfered by the autofluorescence of the biological matrix; (ii) their used single signal mode would engender false positive or negative errors which involved in the instrumental efficiency or some environmental changes during the detection of trace level analytes.

To address the issues, CDs-based ratiometric fluorescent sensors have been developed. Xiao et al. utilized the fluorescence quenching and wavelength conversion of CDs to fabricate a ratiometric fluorescent and colorimetric dual-readout sensor for H2O2 and H2O2-involved metabolites detection [28]. Based on the FRET between CDs and naphthalimide azide derivative, Wu's group design a ratiometric fluorescent sensor for detecting hydrogen sulfide in aqueous media and inside live cells [29]. Recently, CDs-QDs based and CDs-gold nancluster based hybrid nanoprobes have been developed and aroused particular concerns [30,31]. Despite introducing the traditional fluorophores into CDs-based fluorescent probes or sensors can effectively address the above two issues on sensitivity and accuracy, the issue on poor photostability or high toxicity of traditional fluorophores would be brought again at the same time.

Taking the above issues into account, we proposed a novel strategy that of using two types of CDs for constructing the dual-colored CDs-based ratiometric fluorescent sensors. It should be noted that scarcely any related works have been carried out, because it is very hard to seek two compatible CDs which show different emissions under the same excitation light and have no interaction or FRET between them. In this work, a ratiometric fluorescent sensor was achieved based on the difference of FRET between B-CDs-MnO2 system and Y-CDs-MnO2 system. The principle of this strategy was demonstrated in Scheme 1 , B-CDs and Y-CDs were first mixed with MnO2 nanosheets to form the B/Y-CDs-MnO2 nanocomposites via the π-π stacking action, leading to the asynchronous fluorescence quenching of B-CDs and Y-CDs. In the presence of AA that produced by the dephosphorylation between ALP and l-ascorbic acid-2-phosphate (AAP), MnO2 nanosheets would be reduced to Mn2+. As a result, the asynchronous fluorescence recovery related to the concentration of AA emerged and would be used as the indicator to quantify the ALP level or activity. The dual-colored CDs-based ratiometric fluorescent strategy provided more precise measurement without the interference of environmental changes and eliminates ambiguity via self-calibrate of dual readout signals in comparison with single-signal measurement.
The used agents and instruments are described in Supporting Information.

B-CDs were prepared by hydrothermal methods according to the previous literature [32]. Briefly, 0.63 g of citric acid and 0.54 g of ethylenediamine were added into 15 mL of deionized water to form the homogeneous solution under the ultrasonication for 10 min. The prepared solution was transferred into a 25 mL polytetrafluoroethylene (PTFE) autoclave and placed in a blast drying oven for reacting 4 h at 160 °C. Thereafter, the vessel was taken out and naturally cooled to room temperature to obtain the crude product of B-CDs. The crude product of B-CDs were mixed with 60 mL of an ethanol-water solution (Vethanol: Vwater = 8:1) and centrifuged at 8000 rpm for 5 min to remove the unreacted reagents. The precipitate of the lower layer was collected and dissolved in 15 mL of deionized water to obtain pure B-CDs. Y-CDs were synthesized using the solvothermal method according to the reported literature [33]. In brief, 0.15 g of l-glutamic acid and 0.16 g of o-phenylenediamine were firstly added into 25 mL of N, N-dimethylformamide (DMF) followed by being stirred uniformly. Then, the prepared homogeneous solution was transferred into a 50 mL of stainless steel autoclave with a polytetrafluoroethylene (PTFE) tank, and heated at 210 °C for 10 h. After completion of the reaction, the vessel was taken out and naturally cooled to room temperature to obtain the crude Y-CDs dispersion. The obtained product was filtered through a 0.22 μm micropore filter, and the filtrate was centrifuged at 11,000 rpm for 20 min to remove large particles. Subsequently, the supernatant was collected and store at 4 °C for further use.

Ultrathin MnO2 nanosheets were prepared on the basis of the previous report [34]. In a typical reaction, 5 mL of 0.1 mol L−1 sodium dodecylbenzene sulfonate (SDS) and 250 μL of H2SO4 (0.1 mol L−1) were added to 44.25 ml of deionized water followed by being stirred uniformly. The solution was kept stirring at 95 °C for 15 min. Subsequently, 500 μL of KMnO4 solution (0.05 mol L−1) was added into above solution for another 60 min under the same conditions. The brown dispersion was centrifuged in a centrifuge at 10,000 rpm for 15 min. The precipitate was washed three times with deionized water and then dissolved in 5 mL of deionized water.

25 μL of B-CDs dispersions were mixed with 175 μL of Y-CDs dispersions to ensure that their fluorescence peak intensity is comparative. Then, 40 μL of MnO2 nanosheets were added into the above mixture and finaly form the B/Y-CDs-MnO2 nanoprobes after 15 min of interaction with B-CDs and Y-CDs via the π-π stacking.

The dephosphorylation reaction of AAP catalyzed by ALP was carried out under simulated human conditions. As shown in Scheme 1, ALP of Tris-HCl (pH = 8.5) buffer solution with different concentrations were mixed with 10 μL of AAP (0.06 mol L−1) Tris-HCl solution and reacted at 37 °C for 30 min to finally produce AA.

The AA produced by ALP dephosphorylation is mixed with the constructed B/Y-CDs-MnO2 nanoprobes, which can dissolve the MnO2 nanosheets and release the B-CDs and Y-CDs, so that the two CDs have different fluorescence recovery ratios. The reaction equation for the dissolution of MnO2 nanosheets is as follows:

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of ALP activity in aqueous solution
ce:section.ce:para: A series of different concentration gradients of ALP solutions (Tris-HCL buffer, pH 8.5) were prepared at concentrations ranging from 0.1–500 U/L. 180 μL of ALP solution was mixed with 20 μL of AAP (0.6 mmol/L) reacting at 37 °C for 30 min. Then, 150 μL of constructed B/Y-CDs-MnO2 nanoprobes were added into the above reaction solution for 3 min. After the reaction, the fluorescence quenching and recovery were measured.

The blank serum sample was obtained from the ELISA kit. 10 μL of blank serum sample was first diluted 100-fold with Tris-HCL buffer (pH 8.5), and 100 μL of each was taken out and mixed with different concentrations of ALP solution. The fluorescence recovery value was measured by mixing 200 μL of the reaction product (from the dephosphorylation reaction between ALP and AAP) with 150 μL of the B/Y-CDs-MnO2 fluorescent nanoprobes after the incubation of them for 3 min.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of CDs and MnO2 nanosheets
ce:section.ce:para: As shown in Fig. 1 A, B-CDs aqueous dispersion has a strong characteristic absorption peak at ∼345 nm which may attribute to the n-π* transition of CO and π-π* transition of CC [3]. The optimal emission wavelength of the synthesized B-CDs is about 443 nm with a full width at half maximum (FWHM) of ∼70 nm under the optimal excitation wavelength of 345 nm. The smaller FWHM is mainly due to the uniform size distribution of the synthesized B-CDs. The fluorescence excitation spectrum of B-CDs was overlapped with its major UV–vis absorption bands, indicating that these absorptions show relevant structures being responsible for the observed fluorescence emission. As can be seen from the inset, B-CDs with highly water dispersibility show a clear and transparent state of dispersion under visible light and exhibit strong blue fluorescence under 365 nm of UV lights. Fig. 1B shows that Y-CDs exhibit two strong UV absorption peaks at about 378 nm and 399 nm, corresponding to the π-π* transition of CC bond and CN bond [3]. The optimal fluorescence emission wavelength of the synthesized Y-CDs is about 539 nm with the FWHM of ∼100 nm under the optimal excitation wavelength of 414 nm. The wider FWHM of Y-CDs than B-CDs might be caused by their ununiform particle sizes. Y-CDs are clear yellow dispersion under visible light and show strong yellow-green fluorescence at 365 nm excitation of the UV lights. Similarly, a broad UV–vis absorption spectrum from 250 nm to 550 nm and a maximum absorption peak at ∼368 nm of MnO2 nanosheets are in accordance with the reported optical properties of MnO2 nanomaterials (Fig. 1C).

The functional groups of B-CDs and Y-CDs were examined by fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR). Fig. S1 displays that B-CDs have a weak peak at 627 cm−1 due to the skeleton vibration of the C–C ring, while the weak shoulder peak of 1112 cm−1 is attributed to the C–O stretching vibration. The FTIR peaks of B-CDs located at 1392 cm−1 and 1567 cm−1 are caused by the C–N stretching vibration of an amide group and a bending vibration of N–H, respectively. In addition, B-CDs and Y-CDs have a same peak at 3430 cm−1 which is the stretching vibration peak of O–H/N–H. Compared with the peaks of B-CDs, Y-CDs also have a vibration peak of CO at 1660 cm−1 and a weak skeleton vibration peak of C–C ring at 748 cm−1 [29]. The FTIR of MnO2 nanosheets is shown in Fig. S2. The characteristic peak of MnO2 nanosheets is located at 582 cm−1 which is ascribed to the stretching vibration of Mn–O [30]. The wide peak at 3430 cm−1 is corresponded to the stretching vibration of the water molecule ν(HO...H), and a series of peaks at 1568 cm−1, 1398 cm−1 and 1111 cm−1 are attributed to the bending vibration of the H–O bond that combined with Mn.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was employed to characterize the morphology of B-CDs, Y-CDs and MnO2 nanosheets. As shown in Fig. 1D, the B-CDs display the quasi-spherical morphologies with an average size of ∼5 nm and have no large particles or aggregation. The size of Y-CDs is slightly larger than that of B-CDs (∼7 nm) (Fig. 1E). The high-resolution lattice fringes can be observed clearly in the inset of Fig. 1E. The stripe spacing is about 0.210 nm attribute to the (100) lattice distance of graphitic carbon. Fig. 1F shows that the ultrathin MnO2 nanosheets have a wrinkled sheet structure with an average lateral dimension of nearly of ∼500 nm.

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) technique was employed to investigate the elemental composition, elemental chemical states and electronic states of B-CDs and Y-CDs. As it can be seen from the full scan XPS spectra in Fig. 2 A and B, there are three major signal peak located at ∼284, 399 and 531 eV which are assigned to C1s, N1s, and O1s, respectively. It can be clearly known that the B-CDs have larger N/C ratio than Y-CDs. The higher degree N doping can introduce higher density N surface state what are able to facilitate a high yield of radiative recombination and depress non-radiative recombination, leading a higher fluorescence quantum yield (QY). That is why the B-CDs showed higher fluorescence intensity than Y-CDs. There exists four binding energy peaks in B-CDs and Y-CDs which are corresponded to CC/C–C, C–N, C–O, and CO, respectively. They can be divided three types of C including sp2 C (CC), sp3 C (C–C, C–N, and C–O), and oxidized C (CO). Compared with Y-CDs, B-CDs have more C–N band, which agrees well with the higher N doping degree. For N1s spectra of B-CDs, there exist pyrrolic N and graphitic N located at 398.4 and 400.3 eV, respectively. Unlike B-CDs, Y-CDs also have a type of pyridinic N at 399.3 eV [29]. The O1s spectra of B-CDs and Y-CDs comprise two peaks at ∼531.5 and 533.1 eV belonging to C–O and CO bands, respectively. The C–O bands in B-CDs are less than Y-CDs resulting from the role of ethanediamine (EDA) in the formation of carbon skeleton [28].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Investigation of the optical properties of B-CDs, Y-CDs, B/Y-CDs mixture and B/Y-CDs-MnO2
ce:section.ce:para: As we all know, most of CDs exhibit an excitation-dependent fluorescence behavior due to the surface defects and size effect of CDs. Thus, the different wavelengths (340–410 nm) were employed to investigate their excitation-dependent fluorescence behavior. It can be seen from Fig. 3 A that the fluorescence intensity changes greatly with the increase of excitation wavelength from 330 nm to 400 nm, but there is no wavelength shift of B-CDs. The optimal excitation wavelength is 350 nm. On the contrary, both the fluorescence intensity and emission wavelength of Y-CDs varies with the excitation wavelength (Fig. 3B). As the excitation wavelength increases, the peak around 380 nm gradually weakens to disappear, while the peak intensity around 540 nm continually increases. When the excitation wavelength reaches to 380 nm, it can be considered that there is only one peak at 540 nm. After that, the peak position can't be changed with the increase of the excitation wavelength, but the fluorescence intensity is unceasingly enhanced. The differentiation between B-CDs and Y-CDs is mainly caused by that the B-CDs is relatively uniform, while the Y-CDs have large particle size and uneven distribution. Therefore, the optimal excitation wavelength of B-CDs and Y-CDs are 350 nm and 410 nm, respectively.

Before constructing the probe, the effects of excitation wavelength on the B/Y-CDs mixture and the B/Y-CDs-MnO2 nanocomposites should be investigated. In terms of B/Y-CDs mixture, as the excitation wavelength increases, the fluorescence emission peak of B-CDs does not shift, but the fluorescence intensity decreases greatly (Fig. 3C). In contrast, the fluorescence emission peak of Y-CDs has a red shift of ∼5 nm, and the fluorescence intensity enhances with the increase of excitation wavelength until reaching to 375 nm. Considering the fluorescence intensity of B/Y-CDs mixture, 380 nm was chosen as the optimal excitation wavelength. As shown in Fig. 3F, the fluorescence of B/Y-CDs-MnO2 nanocomposites also varied with the excitation wavelength. The peak of B-CDs has a slight red shift with the increase of excitation wavelength. The peaks of Y-CDs have the same optimal emission wavelength at different excitation wavelengths, but the fluorescence intensity increases with the increase of excitation wavelength. To achieve the fluorescence quenching of B-CDs far greater than Y-CDs, 380 nm was also chosen as the optimum excitation wavelength. Similarly, Fig. 3I indicated that the optimum excitation wavelength of B/Y-CDs-MnO2 nanoprobes was 380 nm after adding AA. From the normalization results in Fig. 3D, the FB/FY rises with the increase of B-CDs, but the position of the peak of Y-CDs showed a slight blue shift, because the B-CDs could affect the peak position of Y-CDs. To ensure the equivalent fluorescence intensity of B-CDs and Y-CDs is about and the least influence on fluorescence peak position of Y-CDs, the optimal dosages of B-CDs and Y-CDs are 25 and 175 μL, respectively. The asynchronous fluorescence quenching and recovery can be investigated by overlapping the UV–vis absorption spectrum of MnO2 and the fluorescence emission spectrum of B/Y-CDs mixture (Fig. 3E). It can be clearly observed that the spectral overlap area between B-CDs and MnO2 is about two-fold larger than that between Y-CDs and MnO2 indicating the feasibility of this principle. Furthermore, to achieve maximum efficiency of B/Y-CDs-MnO2 probe, the reaction time between MnO2 and B/Y-CDs and the dosage of MnO2 nanosheets were investigated. It can be seen from Figs. S3a and S3b, the fluorescence intensity of B/Y-CDs reduced clearly after adding MnO2 nanosheets within 15 min. The decrease is reduced after 15 min, which indicates that the reaction reaches equilibrium. As shown in Figs. S4a and S4b, the fluorescence of B/Y-CDs-MnO2 probe is very steady in 15 min to 1 h. Its stability is mainly related to the amino and carboxyl functional groups on the surface of CDs. Thus, it is considered that the probe is stable in 15–60 min and has little effect on the later reaction. Fig. 3G shows the normalized intensity of B/Y-CDs after interacting with different dosages of MnO2. Obviously, the fluorescence peak at 443 nm first decreases with the increase of MnO2 nanosheets and hardly changes with the dosages of MnO2 nanosheets between 30 μL and 60 μL. Abnormally, the fluorescence intensity of the probe increased slightly when the dosages of MnO2 nanosheets increased to 60 μL. In the first stage, the decrease of fluorescence intensity is mainly due to the addition of MnO2 nanosheets. The second stage is the reaction platform demonstrating the completely quench to B/Y-CDs by MnO2 nanosheets. A small fluorescence increase in the third stage may be caused by the overmuch Mn2+ produced by MnO2 nanosheets and AA. The inset shows that the B/Y-CDs-MnO2 probe was excited by UV light of 365 nm with the addition of 0 μL–80 μL of MnO2 nanosheets. It was found that the fluorescence color of the probe changed from yellowish green to yellow gradually with the increase of the amount of MnO2 nanosheets. Therefore, to ensure complete quenching to B/Y-CDs, the optimum dosage of MnO2 nanosheets were chose as 40 μL. Fig. 3H displays that the normalized fluorescence intensity of B/Y-CDs is reduced about 30% after adding MnO2 nanosheets. When a certain concentration of AA was added, the fluorescence can be recovered, and the recovery ratio is about 10%, indicating that the experimental principle of “ture off-on” is reliable. The histogram in Fig. 3H that represents the values of FB/FY also confirms the above conclusion. It can be found that the fluorescence ratio of B/Y-CDs-MnO2 probe is increased by adding AA, mainly because AA can dissolve MnO2 nanoparticles into Mn2+ which can restore the fluorescence of the quenched bicolor CDs. In addition, the optimal reaction time between B/Y-CDs-MnO2 probes and AA were optimized (Figs. S5a and S5b). It can be found that the fluorescence of B/Y-CDs-MnO2 probes can be recovered greatly after adding AA ∼1 min, and can be regarded as complete recovery at about 3 min. The results show that the dissolution reaction between AA and MnO2 nanosheets is very fast and the recovery rate is high. Therefore, it is considered that the recovery time of AA must reach 3 min.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analytical performance of dual-colored based ratiometric fluorescent sensing toward AA
ce:section.ce:para: The effective product of ALP for the dephosphorization of AAP is AA. Therefore, using AA to confirm the feasibility of the method can provide a prerequisite for the quantitative analysis of ALP. The fluorescence of the B/Y-CDs-MnO2 probe was determined after reaction with different concentration of AA for 10 min. The unnormalized fluorescence data was shown in Fig. 4 A. From these spectra, it can be found that the fluorescence recovery of B-CDs is larger than that of Y-CDs. The peak intensities of B-CDs at 443 nm increased with the increase of AA concentrations, while the peak intensities of Y-CDs at 539 nm have no clear change trend with the increase of AA concentration. Thus, the fluorescence data was normalized and shown in Fig. 4D. After normalization, there is a high linear correlation between FB/FY and the logarithm value of AA concentration in the range of 0.1–500 μmol/L (Fig. 4E and F). The equation of linear regression is expressed as FB/FY = 0.12295 × lg CAA + 0.84853 (lg CAA represents the logarithmic value of AA concentration). The square of linear correlation coefficient (R2) is 0.99552. To prove the superior precision of the dual-signal detection mode, Fig. 4B and C present the single-signal detection results and respective calibration curves of B-CDs and Y-CDs. The higher error and signal fluctuation, unsatisfactory LODs and lower R value of single-signal detection indicate the superiority of the ratiometric fluorescence detection mode. Overall, it can be confirmed that B/Y-CDs-MnO2 probe can be used to detect AA and indirectly confirm the feasibility of this probe for the detection of ALP.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analytical performance of dual-colored based ratiometric fluorescent sensing toward ALP
ce:section.ce:para: Before detecting ALP, the conditions of dephosphorization need to be optimized (AAP dosage, pH and reaction time). The AAP dosage was first investigated because the content of AA in the reaction depends on the amount of AAP. If the content of AAP is not high, the dephosphorization reaction of ALP may not be complete and the concentration of AA is not high enough. Conversely, if the content of AAP is too high, the reaction time may be prolonged, which is not conducive to the dephosphorization reaction. Fig. S6 shows that the FB/FY of B/Y-CDs-MnO2 probe increases firstly and then decreases with the increase of AAP content in the reaction solution. The FB/FY of the probe reached the maximum when the AAP was 0.6 mmol/L. It was speculated that the dephosphorization reaction of ALP with this concentration of AAP was more complete and would not interfere with the subsequent reaction. Therefore, the optimal concentration of AAP in the dephosphorization reaction was 0.6 mmol/L. Similarly, the optimal reaction time of AAP in the dephosphorization reaction was 30 min (Fig. S7). Because the pH value of the reaction medium has a significant effect on the enzyme activity, only under the optimum pH value, the dissociation state of the active group on the enzyme molecule is the most suitable for the action of enzyme and substrate, and the enzyme activity is the largest. Fig. S8 indicates that buffer pH had a great influence on the enzyme activity. The maximal FB/FY was obtained when pH was 8.5, which mainly attributed to the more efficient hydrolysis of phosphate ester in alkaline medium. Therefore, the pH of the Tris-HCl buffer added in the dephosphorization reaction was 8.5.

Under the optimal conditions for the dephosphorization of ALP, FB/FY of B/Y-CDs-MnO2 probe gradually increases with increasing the concentration of ALP, indicating a good biocompatibility of the biosensor and an excellent capability in response to target changes (Fig. 5 A). Fig. 5B is the standard calibration curve between the fluorescence ratio of CDs-MnO2 probe and the concentration of ALP. The FB/FY values are proportional to the logarithmic value of the ALP concentrations over a range from 0.1 to 500 U/L. The linear regression equation is FB/FY = 0.06661 × lg CALP + 1.29731 (lg CALP represents the logarithmic value of ALP concentration) with the square of linear correlation coefficient (R2) is 0.99336. The unnormalized fluorescence spectra and fluorescence photos of different concentrations of ALP under UV excitation of 365 nm were shown in Figs. S9 and S10. It can be found that the fluorescence color gradually changed from yellow to yellowish green.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity and stability of dual-colored based ratiometric fluorescent sensors
ce:section.ce:para: In order to determine the unique recognition ability of B/Y-CDs-MnO2 probes for ALP, some similar enzymes and protein were tested at the same concentration as ALP (0.2 mg/L), such as α-amylase (α-Amylase), pepsin, parenzyme and bovine serum albumin (BSA). The results are shown in Fig. 5C. It can be found that pepsin and parenzyme have little effect on the detection of ALP, while high temperature-tolerant α-amylase and bovine serum albumin (BSA) had slight interference with the detection of ALP. These results clearly indicate that the biosensor has highly selectivity and reliability for quantitative analysis of ALP. Furthermore, the biosensor could retain about 90% of the original response after 15 days of storage in 0.01 M Tris-HCl buffer solution at 4 °C.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Standard addition recovery experiments of the ALP in three negative human serums
ce:section.ce:para: As an essential enzyme in human serum, ALP can be used as a biomarker for skeletal diseases, liver dysfunction, heart failure, ovarian cancer and breast cancer. Therefore, it is very important to be able to accurately detect ALP in human serum. The recovery test was performed using the ALP samples with the standard addition method. As shown in Table 1 , different concentrations of ALP (10, 50 and 100 U/L) were mixed with the B/Y-CDs-MnO2 probe in negative human serums. According to the results, it can be found that the recovery rate of ALP is between 91.2% and 108.0% with the RSDS ≤ 3.9%, thereby validating the reliability and practicality of this method.

In summary, this work proposed a novel ratiometric fluorescent sensing strategy based on the dual-colored CDs for achieving high-precision quantitative analysis of AA and ALP. The success construction of the ratiometric sensor is attributed to the following characteristics and designs: (i) B-CDs and Y-CDs as two important emitters showed different emissions under the same excitation light and had no interaction or FRET between them; (ii) MnO2 presented the different quenching effect to B-CDs and Y-CDs; (iii) AA effectively reduce MnO2 to Mn2+, leading to an asynchronous fluorescence recovery; (iv) the ALP, AA, Mn2+ and Tris-buffer have no direct response to B/Y-CDs or/and B/Y-CDs-MnO2 probe. Compared with conventional assays for ALP, the dual colored CDs-based ratiometric fluorescent sensor not only avoid the either poor photostability or high toxicity of traditional fluorescent dyes and quantum dots, but also provided more precise measurement to normalize variation in environmental changes and eliminates ambiguity by cross-reference of two emission bands. Moreover, the sensing strategy can be extended to determine various AA-involved targets only using simply replacing the substrate. Nevertheless, the limitation of this system is that it has only investigated the detection effect of simulated actual sample using a standard addition recovery experiment. Thus, future works should focus on studying their performance and value in practical clinical application.

Kang Shao: Writing - original draft. Ye Yang: Data curation. Shiyi Ye: Methodology. Danyu Gu: Validation. Tao Wang: Software. Yuanjie Teng: Visualization. Zhenlu Shen: Writing - review & editing. Zaifa Pan: Project administration.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We gratefully acknowledge the financial support from National Natural Science Foundation of China (21804119) and Key projects of Zhejiang Natural Science Foundation (Project No. LZ18B050002).

ce:section.ce:para:
The following is the Supplementary data to this article:
